locked_facts: Magnetic field inside solenoid: B = μ₀nI (uniform, parallel to axis)
Field outside solenoid is approximately zero
n = number of turns per unit length
Field direction given by right-hand rule
Solenoid acts like a bar magnet externally
truth_statements: Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic field is uniform and directed along the axis
The field outside a solenoid is approximately zero for an ideal (infinitely long) solenoid
The magnitude of the field inside depends only on the current and the number of turns per unit length, not on the position inside
The direction of the field is determined by the right-hand rule: curl fingers in the direction of current, thumb points to the N-pole direction
A solenoid produces a field pattern identical to a bar magnet when viewed from outside

aha_moment.what_to_show: Rotate the 3D view to show both the dense, parallel field lines inside the solenoid and the very sparse, looping field lines outside. The contrast is dramatic: inside is uniform, outside looks like a bar magnet.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Split attention between inside (uniform, dense, parallel) and outside (sparse, looping from N to S pole). The solenoid IS a bar magnet.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see why the solenoid is called an electromagnet — the external field pattern matches a bar magnet exactly

simulation_states.label: Solenoid coil with current
simulation_states.description: Helical coil visible with current direction shown. No field lines yet. Labels show current direction and coil parameters.
simulation_states.emphasis: This is a solenoid: a tightly wound helical coil carrying current I

simulation_states.label: Internal field lines — dense and uniform
simulation_states.description: Field lines appear inside the solenoid: parallel to axis, equally spaced, all pointing the same direction. B = μ₀nI.
simulation_states.emphasis: Inside: the field is UNIFORM and parallel to the axis. Every field line is straight and equally spaced.

simulation_states.label: External field lines — like a bar magnet
simulation_states.description: Camera pulls back. External field lines visible: they loop from the N-end back to the S-end, just like a bar magnet. Inside is dense, outside is sparse.
simulation_states.emphasis: Outside, the field lines loop from N to S — exactly like a bar magnet! The field is nearly zero far from the solenoid.

simulation_states.label: Explore — full 3D rotation
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode. All elements visible. Student rotates to see the full field pattern from any angle.
simulation_states.emphasis: Rotate: uniform inside, bar-magnet pattern outside. Right-hand rule: curl fingers with current → thumb = N pole.

trigger_keywords: solenoid mein field vary karta hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: The magnetic field inside a solenoid varies from point to point
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: For an ideal (long) solenoid, the magnetic field inside is perfectly uniform: B = μ₀nI at every point inside. It does not depend on position. Only near the ends does the field start to decrease.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show parallel, equally spaced field lines inside — the field is the same everywhere along the axis.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A solenoid with current is fundamentally different from a permanent magnet
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A current-carrying solenoid produces an external magnetic field identical to a bar magnet. It has a north and south pole, and the field lines loop from N to S externally. This is why it is called an electromagnet.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the external field lines — they form the same pattern as a bar magnet.

legend.meaning: Number of turns per unit length

typical_question_types: B inside solenoid with given n and I
field at ends of finite solenoid
common_traps: using total turns N instead of n = N/L
assuming field at end = field at center (it is half)
